speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 3. Joshua Degani is being interviewed. When you look back over this trip from Leipzig, eventually to Palestine, how-- how long in total was the trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trip in total, if you include Yugoslavia, it was about seven months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Seven months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven or eight months altogether.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as you-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But if we don't include it-- so from Yugoslavia, it was a matter of weeks, of a few weeks. I mean, when we left Belgrade, through Saloniki, through Ankara, near the Bosphorus, to Aleppo, and from Aleppo to Beirut, and from Beirut to Rosh HaNikra in Palestine, so it's a matter of a few weeks. But altogether was seven or eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was through the winter, into the spring.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are there personalities, people whom you remember who were particularly hateful or-- or kind during this trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I can remember a number of people who are-- they're very kind as from the time we left. But at this point, I would like to track back to something quite important which had to do with my living at the Hachshara. I was at the Hachshara while events occurred in Leipzig, where my brother and my sisters were, where they had to deliver their possessions, or our possessions, to the Polizei. What happened to me in Steckelsdorf was also not an-- an-- an easy fate because we really produced for the Nazis agricultural items which they needed. And we, of course, had to work extremely hard. It was hard labor. And this came on top of the hard labor which I had to do in Leipzig on the Pflichtarbeit, which I did not also dwell too much because each Jew had to work. And I was lucky also with that, because if I were somewhat older, they-- I would have been sent to a concentration camp. But they had their rules, their own rules, and they said up to this certain date, nobody is taken-- nobody leaves, but they have to do certain kind of Pflichtarbeit, or whatever they called it. And there was a hardship, a terrible hardship, involved. To go into it would take a lot of time itself, to go and to get very little compensation of it. But what little I got could help my mother to survive because my father at this time was already in concentration camp. And whatever little money we got as compensation was quite helpful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the work you were doing at this--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was dirty work. All kinds of cleaning up of furs and all kinds of things which was dirt. And this work was-- Jews were assigned to do all the dirty and-- and-- and negative work there. And we-- I did this for about six months, before I was sent, or before they rescued me, the-- the Religionsgemeinde rescued me for the Hachshara place with another fellow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were living-- you were living apart from your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at that time I came home in dirty clothes every night, every day. I went early to work with the Elektrische, with the trailway, in the morning and came back-- back at night. I lived still with my mother and sister. But this was part of my--

words.text: But this was part of my--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were paid by-- you were paid by the government?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They had a a certain-- a certain way of legal decency, [? which ?] [? is ?] said, some compensation was given for it. You see? And also we can eat. And I felt [? kind ?] that I could work all of a sudden, I was a youngster, that I could do something. At that point, don't forget, I did not feel the terrible hardship. It was hard. It was very hard. But I was relatively strong at that time. I did not feel the terrible hardship, in spite that it-- the effect was that it was misery put on by the authorities for many in my age group. And [? as ?] [? said ?] from there I went to Steckelsdorf. And this was a place near Berlin. Before, I said near Germany. It's near Berlin. What is still memorable was that it was terribly cold when I went there. And we had to work long hours and hard work in all agricultural matters to help the Third Reich. It was not-- not to help with-- not to prepare us for Palestine. It was more-- this is why it was kind of exempted. It was kind of a forced labor in a different name, which it was called at that time Hachshara. So I was there, and it was a terrible place to work. And over there I felt the hardship, more than even before in Leipzig, because it was so miserable. And another hardship, of course, when I escaped, and we escaped, and we came to Zagreb. Before we came to Zagreb, to go over the border was such hardship also, because we had to go over hills and [INAUDIBLE] in all kinds of conditions. And it was a danger by itself. And this also contributed to what I mentioned before, that I wrote my mother that my sister should not-- or my sisters-- should not attempt to do the same because I was afraid of-- of something will happen to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were traveling to-- to Zagreb, that-- that was the difficult part.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was even-- [? I've been-- ?] we were travelling from Graz. Graz was our border town, and I believe in Yugoslavia it was Maribor. This was the other border town where we came. And we had to roll down a big hill-- which was dangerous by itself-- wrapped in-- in-- in kind of wrapped sheets. And coming down the hill was by itself a matter of danger. I mean, rolling down the hill was by itself a danger. And down there it was organized that somebody will wait for us. And somebody waited for an entire group with one car, a small car. And we had-- we were pushed in. We were literally lying one on the other in a small car, and we were 14-- 14 children in that car. But from there, from Maribor, we went to Zagreb.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you-- when you went over the border, this is the way that you made it over the border, was being rolled down the hill?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. This was-- this was one of the hurdles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were not able-- So you were not able to go by train in that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was smooth in that case. From there, we-- don't forget, from there, we went illegally into another country. But from Yugoslavia for-- to Saloniki, to Greece, and from Greece to Turkey, it was a smooth crossing-over. It was not anymore a kind of illegal passing. It was kind-- apparently arranged or pre-arranged by our group leaders to have a smooth ride until we reach Palestine. This is my explanation because the border crossing from German, or from Austria at that time was illegal. But yet when we arrived there and lived there, we were kind of recognized and were living there under the blessing of the authorities when we already were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there non-Jews as well as Jews who were helpful to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were helpful?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now comes the part you asked before, where were we helped. And I told you until the point we reached Syria, Beirut. And from Beirut we went to Rosh HaNikra, which is already Palestine. But the ones who helped were in Turkey. And this is what my memory-- Of course, we were helped before. As I mentioned, the Jews with the [? matzohs ?] in Syria. And we were helped in Yugoslavia by people who were benevolent to us and charitable to us and apparently helped all the way, because we made it. But [BACKGROUND NOISE] one outstanding matter was that when we came to Turkey, or when we left Turkey, that we were given a lot of food into our train. The Jewish congregation sent us all kinds of food from all kinds of factories. What was more than ample supply was cheeses-- hard cheeses. And we as children, in a joking way, threw out the 'ballast,' we called it. It's a ballast because we felt we could not eat so many cheeses.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: There were hundreds of cheeses, large cheeses. And we had other foods also. So we threw it out. And what is memorable-- it-- it sounds funny today, but it was memorable still. We threw out the cheeses. And we said, why should we throw it out just yet when we pass stations where-- where soldiers are stationed, we-- we'll throw it out there. And we really-- there were many soldiers stationed, and we threw it out there. Until some high-authority officer came to our-- our train and said, this you cannot do it. You should not do it. You should not do such thing. And then we were understanding it that we should not do it, and we did not continue. But we arrived safely from there into-- from Turkey, we arrived safety into Aleppo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you still traveling by train?

speaker: interviewee
text: All train. The entire--

speaker: interviewer
text: All train. Just that car picked you up in-- in Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was a car pick-up, which was-- not-- it was a hour or two hours. I don't remember how long it exactly took. But I remember very well that we very-- we were very crowded in that car which picked-- picked us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any was there praying? Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Praying was very much. And you see, we followed-- we were still-- I was very much traditional, you see? As a matter of fact, I insisted on praying. [LAUGHS] I insisted on praying. And if somebody did not pray, I kind of got nervous. And this I remember, and if you would not have asked the question,  I would not have remembered this, even. But there was one guy who took it very easy, but I insisted on praying, and we really prayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the leader of the group.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was the oldest. I could not call myself 'leader.' I was not made [INAUDIBLE] responsible. I was oldest. This is I know because all the others were younger than I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said before that there was one child who was left behind somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this happened in--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that story?

speaker: interviewee
text: This happened in Belgrade. When we were called to the railway station, it was in Zagreb [NON-ENGLISH] in Zagreb. So one child could not make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we never-- he fell sick. He was sick, and we never know what his fate was. We could not-- could not establish what his fate was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able, as such a young boy and in these countries for such short periods of time, to detect any difference in-- in the fears they held for themselves or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all, because we did not deal with anything. We dealt with ourselves. We are preoccupied with ourselves because we had problems. There were problems. I mean, the family was left in Germany, in Leipzig. And we knew that the war was on. We knew a new fate-- something new will come to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were preoccupied, and also with studies. We studied already. We studied already for the new-- I for myself studied English and Hebrew. You see, I knew English, some English, but I tried to perfect myself. And Hebrew was something entirely new which we did not do before. And this was a beginning of a new-- a new lifestyle. How old was the youngest child in this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that the youngest child was five for six years younger, which means I was 14, so the youngest was eight. He later on-- I shouldn't forget when I give you the story-- became a director of a bank. And he is today a director of a bank in Israel. And my fate is-- with Israel now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was in your little satchel that you took on this trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: On this trip we could not take anything. And I don't remember very well some goodwill items-- some-- some items my mother gave me, but it-- there was really nothing-- nothing--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was nothing of-- of financial value, of monetary--

speaker: interviewee
text: We could not-- we did not have-- there was nothing monetary, as far as I remember. We went with our naked bodies. This was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a-- did you have a tallis or a yarmulke or any religious--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yarmulke, yes, because we prayed, so apparently this was there. But religious-- if there were-- I think there were also tefillin. I think there were also tefillin, because we already were-- at the bar mitzvah, there was-- yes, there were tefillin. Yes, there was. Yes, there was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any-- any sentimental item?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some pictures, yes. Pictures were with us. Pictures from my father, which I carry still with me, and my mother, my sisters. At that time, these were with me, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, and when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: And [INAUDIBLE] we had some writing paper. We had. Because this was one of my points, to have always something to write. This I had with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you writing along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will-- I keep--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you keeping a diary of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not diary, but I wrote--

speaker: interviewer
text: A journal?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote-- I wrote information, which I related later on when we reached Zagreb. I wrote to my mother and to my grandmother and to my sisters. I mean, this was all in one postcard or one letter, which we could do and did so lovingly. And my mother write-- wrote constantly. And I have her postcards still with me, and they're well-preserved after 53, 55 years, as if they were written yesterday. They have the sign of the Hakenkreuz, which shows that they were all censored. I had many more, but they were lost. Right now I have about five or six postcards left with me. They apparently will end up in one of the museums, or at least part of them will be in one of the museums.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that, as a Jewish child, that you were on a mission of some kind, that you-- that you were-- you were chosen to be saved, for any particular reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were-- we always believed in God, you see, and we trusted that God will do the right thing with us. And perhaps this gave us the strength that we could maintain ourselves, that we could go forward, in spite of what came over us or what we later became unaware. And in Zagreb it was that I became aware that my father was murdered in Dachau. This happened in--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned it because the rabbi of the synagogue where we went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Zagreb?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Zagreb-- told us that my father died in the concentration camp, and he got notification. The fact was that my relative got a letter from my mother, but he did not tell us. He told it to the rabbi. And the rabbi told us when we went there for a prayer. He told us this, and he said, you cannot sit any more shiva but one hour, because it happened already some time ago. And if it's not in the same week, so it is only one hour where you sit shiva. And so we sat shiva. But we nevertheless did not to go to movies. We did not go to anything which was entertainment. We saw this as kind of holy matter which we must observe to respect the memory of our murdered father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you understand at that time what a concentration camp was about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not exactly. I knew that bad things happened. But again, coming back to the Schutzhaft, I was always, at that time or before, it was still the impression that he was kind-- in protective custody. 'Schutzhaft' means 'under schutz'-- 'protected'-- by the Nazis. But at that time, it was already clear that these things like concentration camps, where people are dying, is happening. I did not know about all the places where it occurred. But I knew that it occurred with my father. And later on I became aware also that-- what happened to my mother and my-- my-- my sisters. And I obtained documentation in Israel about my mother. And they wrote me, and I have it and framed, even, that my mother and three sisters were-- [GERMAN]-- which means '1902,' in Vizhnitz, and her children-- [GERMAN]-- Miriam Habergrutz, Rosa Habergrutz, Mendel Habergrutz, [GERMAN]-- when they're-- they're born. [GERMAN]-- on 21st of January, 1942, to Riga deportiert. They were deported to Riga. And since there was no sign of life after that, so we can take-- take it as assurance-- 'Sicherheit'-- we can take it as assurance that they are not anymore alive and that they were-- [GERMAN]-- that their-- that their life does not exist anymore. And this was my mother and sister-- sisters. And my grandmother was at a later date also deported, but to another place, which was Belzec in Poland, in Lublin, near Lublin, in Poland. And this happened to [GERMAN] that Mrs. Hannah [NON-ENGLISH] [GERMAN] [NON-ENGLISH] [GERMAN] on 30th-- on the 30th of August in 1875 in Vizhnitz, which is also in Poland. In-- on 10th of May in 1942, [GERMAN]-- to-- where was deported to [NON-ENGLISH]. And said she was a [INAUDIBLE] of the Israelitische-- that she was a member of the Israelitische Gemeinde-- so was my mother-- and she had to wear also at that time the Judenstern, the Judenstern. And that she was living at that time in this Judenhaus, which was a ghetto of the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this occurred at 1942 in January, and my grandmother in May [INAUDIBLE]. She also was correctly assumed that because of her age and of the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: But what was even worse what I became aware how this happened and what this meant to be deported. This was the most horrible act in a lifetime to occur to a family. It was a night which was 17 below zero. And when they arrived at Riga, after they were hit and injured and dreadfully treated. And they arrived at Riga, it was double-cold. It was 33 below zero. And it was a most horrible thing, as I learned from a description from one who survived. And-- although there, they came from many cases, not only Leipzig. It was from many other places in Germany that the deportations took place. Also to Riga and to other places, to Riga, and Riga is in Lithuania. And over there was a concentration camp. I cannot call it differently-- perhaps they call it 'ghetto'-- with-- where 30,000 Jews died. And some were rescued. Some were helped. And perhaps with the assistance of the people who-- some people who lived there. And they could escape to, as described, to Danzig, which was a satellite of Poland at that time-- was independent later. This was also taken by the Nazis. But at that time, they could make it. Or it was delivered.

speaker: interviewer
text: I am not sure what really happened with Danzig at that time. But we are sure what happened with France and with Holland and with Denmark. And, of course, the beginning-- Poland and in Italy and Greece and other countries. Any other questions with regard to this point?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, this is that they had to-- what they did over there with this group which arrived in Riga was that they were forced to walk long distances in this cold, dreadful weather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sisters were very little girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sisters were very little girls. And I don't know how and what-- how they arrived and if they arrived in the Lager, which was also a dreadful place to live. But these details I am not aware. But the details I am aware of are the most horrible picture I could conceive in my lifetime. And thinking back how dreadful it was for my father to die in Dachau, after all the descriptions and after all I read, this may have been equally dreadful over there. And you really cannot take measure what was worse because all was a terrible thing which happened under the Nazi regime. I have pictures of my sisters and documents which show--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said a neighbor-- a former-- somebody who was there with them and who survived related this story to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was related in a book, not a neighbor we knew. A-- somebody who lived there and who I did not know and do not know and still don't know related a story about what happened to the [? Todestransport ?] to Riga, to Riga [GERMAN]. Somebody related it in a story. This is where from I learned these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you learn about your-- your mother and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: When this book was published, I-- a friend of Leipzig who lives in New York told me about this book. I ordered it from the Israelitische Religionsgemeinde. They send it to me, and here I read all the stories, which is-- which are authentic, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you suffer from the cold on your trip to-- to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suffered from the cold when I encountered it at the Hachshara place which I mentioned-- Steckelsdorf near Berlin. I encountered the cold when I was in Pflichtarbeit and Zwangsarbeit in Leipzig. It was cold, very cold. But nothing, of course, as my mother and sisters and my father encountered in the concentration camps. I or we stayed alive. We overcame all this. They were not given the chance or opportunity to overcome it because they were murdered brutally, in a most brutal way, murdered-- murdered. All was taken and murdered. All was taken. They were murdered.
